attributes.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a six month history of recurrent episodes of right upper quadrant pain after meals. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The abdominal examination is unremarkable. An ultrasound reveals a thickened gallbladder wall and the radiologists reports the presence of Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following correctly describes Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses?
options: Abnormal connections between bile ducts and pancreatic ducts
Congenital cysts within the gallbladder fundus
Dilated lymphatic channels in the gallbladder serosa
Inflammatory infiltrates in the gallbladder muscularis
Invaginations of mucosa through the gallbladder wall
attributes.explanation:
Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses are a characteristic histological finding in gallbladder pathology, particularly in adenomyomatosis and chronic cholecystitis. They are occasionally seen on imaging, where small cystic spaces within the wall are noted. Invaginations of mucosa through the gallbladder wall accurately describes these sinuses, which represent herniation of the epithelium through the muscular layer of the gallbladder wall.

Dilated lymphatic channels in the gallbladder serosa is incorrect. While lymphatic channels exist in the gallbladder wall, they are distinct from Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses and are not typically visible on imaging.

Abnormal connections between bile ducts and pancreatic ducts describes pancreaticobiliary maljunction, which is an entirely different anatomical abnormality that can lead to various complications including biliary cancer.

Congenital cysts within the gallbladder fundus is incorrect. While gallbladder cysts can occur, Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses are acquired rather than congenital and can occur throughout the gallbladder wall.

Inflammatory infiltrates in the gallbladder muscularis may be seen in cholecystitis but does not describe Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses. While inflammation may be present around these sinuses, it is not their defining characteristic.

On ultrasound, Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses appear as small cystic spaces within a thickened gallbladder wall, which is characteristic of adenomyomatosis. Their presence can help differentiate adenomyomatosis from other gallbladder pathologies. Understanding these sinuses is important as they can be associated with chronic gallbladder disease and may influence surgical decision-making.
learningPoints: Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses are outpouchings of gallbladder mucosa through the muscular wall, commonly seen in chronic cholecystitis.
The presence of Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses is a characteristic histological finding in adenomyomatosis of the gallbladder.
These sinuses can be seen on ultrasound as small cystic spaces within a thickened gallbladder wall.
references: Sharma S, Walia BS, Randhawa M, Sharma A, Dugg P, Pannu JS. Histopathological changes in gall bladder mucosa in relation to the number, and size of gallstones, and analysis of the findings in the context of age distribution of the patients: A perspective. Ann Hepatobiliary Pancreat Surg. 2023 Aug 31;27(3):277-286. doi: 10.14701/ahbps.23-010. Epub 2023 Aug 7. PMID: 37547937; PMCID: PMC10472125.

Singh A, Singh G, Kaur K, Goyal G, Saini G, Sharma D. Histopathological Changes in Gallbladder Mucosa Associated with Cholelithiasis: A Prospective Study. Niger J Surg. 2019 Jan-Jun;25(1):21-25. doi: 10.4103/njs.NJS_15_18. PMID: 31007507; PMCID: PMC6452760.

Ginanni Corradini S, Yamashita G, Nuutinen H, Chernosky A, Williams C, Hays L, Shiffman ML, Walsh RM, Svanvik J, Della Guardia P, Capocaccia L, Holzbach RT. Human gallbladder mucosal function: effects on intraluminal fluid and lipid composition in health and disease. Dig Dis Sci. 1998 Feb;43(2):335-43. doi: 10.1023/a:1018858406560. PMID: 9512127.
Nightingale, J. M. (2012). Applied anatomy and physiology of the gastrointestinal tract (GIT). Gastrointestinal Tract Imaging E-Book: Gastrointestinal Tract Imaging E-Book, 45.
Podolsky, D. K., Camilleri, M., Fitz, J. G., Kalloo, A. N., Shanahan, F., & Wang, T. C. (Eds.). (2015). Yamada's textbook of gastroenterology. John Wiley & Sons.
